event: Neolithic farming communities established the Jeitun culture, among the earliest agricultural settlements in Central Asia, near present-day Ashgabat.
The Bronze Age Bactria-Margiana Archaeological Complex flourished in the Murghab river delta, with the major urban center of Gonur Depe.
Iranian-speaking nomadic and settled peoples spread across the region, laying the foundations of later Khwarezmian and Parthian cultures.
The region was incorporated into the Achaemenid Persian Empire as parts of the satrapies of Margiana, Parthia, and Khwarezm.
Alexander the Great conquered the territory during his campaign against the Achaemenid Empire, founding Hellenistic settlements.
The Parni nomads established the Parthian Empire in the region around Nisa, near modern Ashgabat.
Nisa became a royal residence and ceremonial capital of the Parthian Arsacid dynasty.
The Sasanian Persian Empire overthrew the Parthians and absorbed the territory of present-day Turkmenistan.
Arab Muslim armies conquered Merv and the surrounding regions, beginning the Islamization of the territory.
Merv served as a base for the Abbasid Revolution led by Abu Muslim against the Umayyad Caliphate.
The Tahirid dynasty, followed by Persianate states, governed Khorasan including Merv under nominal Abbasid suzerainty.
The Samanid Empire incorporated the region, fostering a flourishing of Persian and Islamic culture.
The Oghuz Turkic Seljuk dynasty, ancestors of the Turkmen, rose to power and made Merv a major capital.
Sultan Sanjar made Merv the capital of the Great Seljuk Empire, and it became one of the largest cities in the world.
The death of Sultan Sanjar marked the decline of Seljuk power, and his mausoleum at Merv became an architectural landmark.
Mongol armies under Genghis Khan's son Tolui sacked Merv, massacring much of its population and devastating the region.
Timur (Tamerlane) brought the region under the Timurid Empire centered on nearby Samarkand.
Following Timurid decline, Turkmen tribes lived under the contested influence of the Safavid Persians and Uzbek Khanates.
Turkmen tribal confederations such as the Teke, Yomut, and Ersari dominated the steppes and oases between Persia and Khiva.
Nader Shah of Persia campaigned through Turkmen lands, asserting Persian control over parts of the region.
The Khanate of Khiva and the Emirate of Bukhara competed for control over Turkmen tribes along the Amu Darya.
Russia founded Krasnovodsk (now Turkmenbashi) on the Caspian coast, beginning its military advance into Turkmen territory.
Russian forces under General Skobelev captured the Teke fortress of Geok Tepe, killing thousands and breaking Turkmen resistance.
Russia annexed the Merv oasis, completing the conquest of Turkmen lands and incorporating them into the Transcaspian Oblast.
The Panjdeh incident nearly sparked war between Russia and Britain over the southern Turkmen frontier with Afghanistan.
The Transcaspian Railway reached Samarkand, transforming Ashgabat and the region into a strategic transport corridor.
Turkmen joined the broader Central Asian revolt against Russian wartime conscription and colonial rule.
The Russian Revolution triggered upheaval, and anti-Bolshevik forces seized control of Transcaspia.
The Transcaspian Provisional Government, backed by British troops, fought the Bolsheviks during the Russian Civil War.
The Red Army defeated anti-Bolshevik forces and established firm Soviet control over Transcaspia.
The Turkmen Soviet Socialist Republic was created as a constituent republic of the Soviet Union through national delimitation.
The Turkmen SSR was formally admitted as a full union republic of the USSR with Ashgabat as its capital.
Soviet collectivization of agriculture and forced settlement of nomadic Turkmen began, meeting fierce Basmachi resistance.
A catastrophic earthquake destroyed Ashgabat, killing an estimated 110,000 people in one of the deadliest quakes on record.
Construction of the Karakum Canal began, eventually creating one of the world's longest irrigation canals across the desert.
Saparmurat Niyazov became First Secretary of the Communist Party of the Turkmen SSR, beginning his long dominance.
The Turkmen Supreme Soviet declared sovereignty, and Niyazov was elected the republic's first president.
Turkmenistan declared independence from the Soviet Union following a referendum, with Niyazov as president.
Turkmenistan joined the United Nations and adopted a new constitution concentrating power in the presidency.
Niyazov adopted the title Turkmenbashi, meaning 'Leader of the Turkmen,' as the nation introduced its own currency, the manat.
The United Nations formally recognized Turkmenistan's policy of permanent neutrality.
Parliament declared Niyazov president for life, cementing one of the world's most repressive personality cults.
Niyazov published the Ruhnama, a spiritual guidebook made mandatory in schools, workplaces, and public life.
President Saparmurat Niyazov died suddenly, ending more than two decades of authoritarian rule.
Gurbanguly Berdimuhamedow was elected president, gradually softening some of Niyazov's most extreme policies.
The Central Asia-China gas pipeline opened, making China the dominant export market for Turkmenistan's vast natural gas reserves.
Construction began on the Galkynysh gas field development, one of the largest natural gas fields in the world.
Work began on the TAPI pipeline intended to carry Turkmen gas to Afghanistan, Pakistan, and India.
Ashgabat hosted the Asian Indoor and Martial Arts Games, showcasing the marble-clad capital to the world.
Serdar Berdimuhamedow won the presidential election, succeeding his father in a dynastic transfer of power.
